Output:
PR: mise install --monorepo + unified monorepo lockfile
Context
Implements both features agreed in discussion #10667: (1) a way to install the union of tools across all monorepo config_roots (CI cache warming), and (2) a single <monorepo_root>/mise.lock instead of per-subproject lockfiles (jdx: subproject locks "are a bug"). Monorepo mode is experimental, so no extra gating is needed. Design decisions settled in conversation: --monorepo flag (not //... target syntax), active-MISE_ENV-only union, upsert-only writes from partial views, prune only on full-view ops, migrate-and-delete stray subproject locks, explicit [monorepo].config_roots required for full-view ops.

Verified critical finding: the existing "contributing sources" logic in update_lockfiles is NOT sufficient for a shared root lock. merge_tool_entries (lockfile.rs:1941-1984) returns only by_key.into_values() — existing entries whose (version, options) key is absent from the current write are dropped (line 1444 replaces the tool's whole Vec). Without an upsert-only fix, an install in sub-a (node@20) would delete sub-b's node@22 from the root lock.

Branch / PR
New branch off up-to-date main: monorepo-install-unified-lock.
PR title: feat(monorepo): add install --monorepo and unified root lockfile.
PR body: link discussion #10667, explain both features + the upsert/prune model, note migration behavior; standard Claude Code footer.
Step 1 — ToolRequestSetBuilder::with_config_files
src/toolset/tool_request_set.rs: add config_files: Option<ConfigMap> field + fluent setter (mirroring ToolsetBuilder at builder.rs:59-62). In load_config_files (tool_request_set.rs:204-213) use self.config_files.as_ref().unwrap_or(&config.config_files). This reuses env-var/arg logic verbatim per root.

Step 2 — Union helpers on Config (src/config/mod.rs, near monorepo_root() :400)
pub fn monorepo_config_root_dirs(&self) -> Result<Vec<PathBuf>>: via find_monorepo_config (:927); error if not in a monorepo ("no config file in scope sets monorepo_root = true"); error if [monorepo].config_roots missing/empty (walking discovery is deprecated); expand via expand_config_roots(root, patterns, None) (:2083). Do NOT use discover_monorepo_subdirs' walking fallback.
Extract private config_paths_in_dir(dir) from the identical inline logic in load_local_tasks_with_context (:1986-2008; DEFAULT_CONFIG_FILENAMES already covers active-MISE_ENV variants) and reuse in both places.
pub async fn monorepo_union_config_files(self: &Arc<Self>) -> Result<ConfigMap>: clone self.config_files, entry().or_insert each subdir config (the run.rs:363-368 pattern). For full-view iteration (mise lock, install's not-activated warning).
pub async fn monorepo_union_tool_request_set(self: &Arc<Self>) -> Result<ToolRequestSet>:
Start from self.get_tool_request_set().await?.clone() (active hierarchy), with the current subproject's configs stripped from the base set.
Per config root: ConfigMap of base files + that root's configs (ordered so subdir wins — verify against .rev() iteration; unit-test the override chain), parse via load_config_files_from_paths (:1627), build with ToolRequestSetBuilder::new().with_config_files(...). Per-root precedence means MISE_ENV overrides replace base versions instead of unioning them.
Union across roots: add_version only if no existing request with equal (version(), options()) for that backend — preserves distinct versions (node@20 + node@22) while deduping identical requests. A plain ToolRequestSet::merge would collapse per-tool and is wrong here.
Step 3 — CLI flags
src/cli/install.rs: #[clap(long, env = "MISE_MONOREPO", verbatim_doc_comment)] monorepo: bool with doc comment (union across config_roots, active MISE_ENV, CI cache warming, requires monorepo_root + explicit config_roots). Both paths swap config.get_tool_request_set() for the union helper when set — install_runtimes (:203, positional args filter the union → --monorepo node installs every node request) and install_missing_runtimes (:400, bare = everything). The "installed but not activated" warning (:245-251) scans monorepo_union_config_files() when the flag is set.
src/cli/ls.rs: same flag; swap :269 to the union helper (~10 lines; CI cache keys).
Outside a monorepo or missing config_roots → clear errors from the Step 2 helper.
Step 4 — Lockfile routing (src/lockfile.rs)
lockfile_path_for_config(config_path, monorepo_root: Option<&Path>) (:1154): if Some(root), config_path.starts_with(root), and lockfile_dir != root → (root.join(lockfile_name), is_local). Variant names derive from the config filename, so sub/mise.ci.toml → <root>/mise.ci.lock, sub/mise.local.toml → <root>/mise.local.lock; conf.d handled before routing; global/system configs never match.
Thread config.monorepo_root() through callers: lockfile_path_for_tool_source (:1187, both branches), update_lockfiles (:1305), read_all_lockfiles (:2006 — seen_roots dedupe then collapses subprojects to one root read), read_lockfile_for (:2063, param passed from get_locked_version :2100), src/toolset/mod.rs:698, src/cli/lock.rs:485, unit tests (:2709 pass None).
src/backend/conda.rs:368: switch read_lockfile_for_tool to lockfile_path_for_tool_source(&ctx.config, ...) (call site :495 has ctx), mirroring pkgx.rs:194.
Step 5 — Upsert-only writes + fslock (update_lockfiles, :1262)
Call migrate_monorepo_lockfiles(config) (Step 6) after the enabled-check when in a monorepo.
Per-lockfile loop: when the target is the monorepo root lock, after merge_tool_entries (:1443) re-append existing entries whose (version, options) key is absent from the merged set (then re-sort) — a subproject install never removes siblings' entries. cleanup_unreferenced_conda_packages reads lockfile contents, so preserved entries keep their packages.
Wrap each lockfile's read→save in crate::lock_file::LockFile (fslock, lock_file.rs:11-46) so concurrent subproject installs serialize on the shared root lock.
Documented consequence: plain installs upsert, so stale versions linger in the root lock until mise lock prunes — by design.
Step 6 — Migration (migrate_monorepo_lockfiles in src/lockfile.rs)
No-op unless lockfile enabled + in monorepo; if monorepo_config_root_dirs() errors (no config_roots), return Ok silently.
Per config-root dir (excluding root itself): find ^mise(\.[^.]+)?(\.local)?\.lock$ files; for each, under fslock on the corresponding root variant: Lockfile::read root variant (returns default for missing → creates it, preserving pins), merge subproject entries with root-wins-per-(short, version, options) (append-if-absent), merge conda/pkgx tables likewise, save, delete subproject file.
One notice line: migrated {n} subproject lockfile(s) into {root} (monorepo uses a single root lockfile).
Call sites: update_lockfiles and Lock::run (first, before resolution, so resolution reads the migrated lock).
Step 7 — mise lock full-view mode (src/cli/lock.rs)
When config.is_monorepo() (automatic, no flag — in a monorepo the config root IS the monorepo root, and a partial-view prune would destroy sibling entries):

migrate_monorepo_lockfiles first.
monorepo_union_config_files() (errors without config_roots — prevents unsafe prune).
Build the toolset in both branches (:91-99) via ToolsetBuilder::new().with_config_files(union_files).
Parameterize config_paths_in_lock_scope (:430), get_lockfile_targets (:472), get_tools_to_lock second pass (:554) with effective_config_files: &ConfigMap (defaults to config.config_files). In scope resolution add a monorepo branch: all non-global paths under the monorepo root (the existing config_root == target_root check would drop subproject configs).
Unfiltered prune (:128) is then safe. compute_config_bumps_for_paths skips union-only paths harmlessly. --local keeps working via is_local from filenames.
Tests
Unit (lockfile.rs mod tests ~:2391): routing (sub base/local/env/conf.d → root variants; outside-root and root-own unrouted); upsert preservation of absent-key entries; migration merge (root wins on conflict, env-variant files → env-variant root locks). Unit (config/tool_request_set): union keeps node@20+node@22 across roots; within-root override chain not unioned.

E2E (run via mise run test:e2e <name>; MISE_EXPERIMENTAL=1, tiny tool, helpers from e2e/assert.sh):

e2e/cli/test_install_monorepo: sub-a tiny@1, sub-b tiny@2+dummy; bare --monorepo installs all; --monorepo tiny installs both tiny versions only; assert_fail_contains outside monorepo and with missing config_roots.
e2e/lockfile/test_lockfile_monorepo (MISE_LOCKFILE=1): touch root mise.lock; installs in sub-a then sub-b both land in root lock, no sub-*/mise.lock created; upsert keeps sibling entries; removing a tool from a config + mise lock prunes it.
e2e/lockfile/test_lockfile_monorepo_migration: pre-created sub-a/mise.lock + sub-a/mise.ci.lock merge into root variants, files deleted, notice printed.
Docs
docs/tasks/monorepo.md: new "Tools" section (install --monorepo, ls --monorepo, CI example with MISE_ENV) and "Lockfile" section (single root lock, migration, mise lock is monorepo-wide, upsert-vs-prune model).
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md: short monorepo cross-link section.
mise run render:usage regenerates docs/cli/{install,ls}.md.
Verification
mise run build; cargo test lockfile + cargo test config; mise run snapshots (insta); mise run test:e2e test_install_monorepo test_lockfile_monorepo test_lockfile_monorepo_migration; mise run lint-fix (+ git add fixes) before committing. Then push branch and gh pr create (title above; body links discussion #10667), report URL in <pr-created> tags.

Deferred (note in PR body)
--monorepo on upgrade/prune/outdated (helper ready); args-path lockfile retention gap (install.rs:296); idiomatic version files in config roots contributing to the union; fslock around cli/lock.rs writes; read-side auto-migration.

payload.message: I’ve confirmed the current `ToolRequestSetBuilder` cannot yet be pointed at an alternate `ConfigMap`, while `ToolsetBuilder` can. That explains why the union helper in the note starts by adding a sibling `with_config_files` API rather than inventing a new parse path.

payload.message: The lock command already treats an unfiltered `mise lock` as a prune operation. For monorepo root locks, that means the plan has to make `mise lock` operate on the full configured monorepo view automatically; otherwise it would prune sibling entries by accident.

docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:3:`mise.lock` is a lockfile that pins exact versions and checksums of tools for reproducible environments. Lockfiles are not created automatically—you must run `mise lock` to generate them. Once a lockfile exists, mise will keep it updated as tools are installed or upgraded.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:7:The lockfile serves similar purposes to `package-lock.json` in npm or `Cargo.lock` in Rust:
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:12:- **Avoids API rate limits**: By storing download URLs, future installs use the lockfile and do not need to call GitHub (or other providers), avoiding rate limits and the need for `GITHUB_TOKEN` in most cases

Environment-specific lockfiles are strictly scoped to their corresponding config — they only contain tools defined in that config.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:121:Tools defined in `mise.local.toml` (which is typically gitignored) use a separate `mise.local.lock` file. This keeps local tool configurations separate from the committed lockfile.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:131:Use `mise lock --local` to update the local lockfile for all platforms:
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:140:The `locked` setting enforces that all tools have pre-resolved URLs in the lockfile before installation. This prevents API calls to GitHub, aqua registry, etc., ensuring fully reproducible installations.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:151:All mise settings are global in scope. Setting `locked = true` in a project's `mise.toml` applies to **all** tool resolution, including tools from your global `~/.config/mise/config.toml`. If you see warnings about global tools missing from the lockfile, run `mise lock -g` to generate a global lockfile.

docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:340:When `mise lock` generates a lockfile, it records a verified provenance type (e.g., `slsa`, `cosign`, `minisign`, `github-attestations`) for each tool when one is available. For the **current platform**, mise downloads the artifact and performs full cryptographic verification at lock time -- ensuring the provenance entry in the lockfile is backed by actual verification, not just registry metadata. This applies to both the aqua and github backends. For cross-platform entries, provenance is detected from registry metadata without verification (since the artifact may not be runnable on the current machine).
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:342:By default, when `mise install` sees a lockfile with both a checksum and a verified provenance entry, it trusts the lockfile and skips re-verification. This avoids redundant API calls (e.g., GitHub attestation queries) which can cause rate limit issues in CI. Since the current platform's provenance was already verified during `mise lock`, this is safe.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:344:If GitHub Artifact Attestations are enabled but the GitHub API confirms none exist for a checksum-backed artifact, mise may record `github_attestations = "unavailable"`. This is a negative cache entry, not provenance: it only skips the redundant GitHub attestation probe on later installs from that lockfile. Other verification paths such as SLSA, Cosign, Minisign, and checksum verification still run as usual.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:346:GitHub's docs show binary attestations generated from an existing artifact path with [`actions/attest`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/use-artifact-attestations/use-artifact-attestations#generating-build-provenance-for-binaries), and the REST API lists attestations by [subject digest](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/orgs/attestations#list-attestations). That means an attestation can appear after the release asset was uploaded. A later `mise lock` run or `MISE_LOCKED_VERIFY_PROVENANCE=1 mise install` can discover attestations added after the lockfile recorded them as unavailable.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:368:When enabled, every `mise install` will cryptographically verify provenance regardless of what the lockfile contains, ensuring the artifact was built by a trusted CI pipeline.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:372:In addition to lockfiles, mise uses the [`minimum_release_age`](/configuration/settings.html#minimum_release_age) setting to limit supply chain risk by only installing versions that have been available for a minimum amount of time. It defaults to `24h`:
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:379:This pairs well with lockfiles — use `minimum_release_age` to avoid picking up brand-new releases, and lockfiles to pin the exact versions you've vetted.

Chunk ID: bb83d0 Wall time: 0.0000 seconds Process exited with code 0 Original token count: 2195 Output: 1 # Monorepo Tasks 2 3 mise supports monorepo-style task organization with target path syntax. This feature allows you to manage tasks across multiple projects in a single repository, where each project can have its own `mise.toml` configuration with tools, environment variables, and tasks that may be different from where the task is called from. 4 5 ## Overview 6 7 When `monorepo_root` is enabled in your root `mise.toml`, mise will automatically discover tasks in subdirectories and prefix them with their relative path from the monorepo root. This creates a unified task namespace across your entire repository. 8 9 ::: tip 10 The directory containing a `mise.toml` file is called the **config_root**. In monorepo mode, each project can have its own config_root with its own configuration, separate from the monorepo root. Note that if you use one of the alternate paths in a subdirectory like `./projects/frontend/.mise/config.toml`, the config_root will be `./projects/frontend`–not `./projects/frontend/.mise`. 11 ::: 12 13 ### Benefits 14 15 - **Consistent execution**: Run tasks from any location in the monorepo using the mise config that would be set if called from the task's directory 16 - **Clear task namespacing**: Tasks are prefixed with their location from the monorepo root 17 - **Pattern-based execution**: Use wildcards to run tasks across multiple projects 18 - **Tool and environment layering**: Subdirectory tasks use tools and environment variables from parent configs, but can also define their own in their config_root 19 - **Automatic trust propagation**: When the monorepo root is trusted, all descendant configs are automatically trusted 20 21 ## Configuration 22 23 ### Enabling Monorepo Mode 24 25 Add `monorepo_root = true` to your root `mise.toml`: 26 27 ```toml 28 # /myproject/mise.toml 29 monorepo_root = true 30 31 [tools] 32 # Tools defined here apply to all subdirectories 33 node = "20" 34 ``` 35 36 ### Example Structure 37 38 ``` 39 myproject/ 40 ├── mise.toml (with monorepo_root = true) 41 ├── projects/ 42 │ ├── frontend/ 43 │ │ └── mise.toml (with tasks: build, test) 44 │ └── backend/ 45 │ └── mise.toml (with tasks: build, test) 46 ``` 47 48 With this structure, tasks will be automatically namespaced: 49 50 - `//projects/frontend:build` 51 - `//projects/frontend:test` 52 - `//projects/backend:build` 53 - `//projects/backend:test` 54 55 ## Task Path Syntax 56 57 Monorepo tasks use special path syntax with `//` and `:` prefixes. You can run these tasks directly with `mise` or with `mise run`. With non-monorepo tasks, the guidance is to avoid using the direct syntax for scripts because it could conflict with future core mise commands. However, mise will never define commands with a `//` or `:` prefix, so this guidance does not apply to monorepo tasks. 58 59 ```bash 60 # Direct syntax (preferred for monorepo tasks) 61 mise //projects/frontend:build 62 63 # Also works with 'run' 64 mise run //projects/frontend:build 65 66 # Need quotes for wildcards 67 mise '//projects/frontend:*' 68 ``` 69 70 ### Absolute Paths 71 72 Use `//` prefix to specify the absolute path from the monorepo root: 73 74 ```bash 75 # Run build task in frontend project 76 mise //projects/frontend:build 77 78 # Run test task in backend project 79 mise //projects/backend:test 80 ``` 81 82 ### Current config_root Tasks 83 84 Use `:` prefix to run tasks in the current config_root: 85 86 ```bash 87 cd projects/frontend 88 mise :build # Runs the build task from frontend's config_root 89 ``` 90 91 ::: tip Optional Colon Syntax 92 The leading `:` is optional when running tasks from subdirectories or defining task dependencies. While both syntaxes work, **we encourage using the `:` prefix to be explicit** about monorepo task references. 93 94 **Running from subdirectory:** 95 96 ```bash 97 cd projects/frontend 98 mise :build # Recommended: Explicit monorepo task reference 99 mise build # Also works (for migration compatibility) 100 ``` 101 102 **Task dependencies:** 103 104 ```toml 105 # projects/frontend/mise.toml 106 [tasks.lint] 107 run = "eslint ." 108 109 [tasks.build] 110 depends = [":lint"] # Recommended: Explicit and clear 111 # OR 112 depends = ["lint"] # Also works (for migration compatibility) 113 run = "webpack build" 114 ``` 115 116 The bare name syntax (without `:`) is supported primarily to ease migration from non-monorepo to monorepo configurations. When migrating, you won't need to update all your task dependencies immediately - they'll continue to work. However, using the `:` prefix makes it clear you're referencing a task in the current config_root. 117 ::: 118 119 ### Wildcard Patterns 120 121 mise supports two types of wildcards for flexible task execution: 122 123 #### Path Wildcards (`...`) 124 125 Use ellipsis (`...`) to match any directory depth: 126 127 ```bash 128 # Run 'test' task in ALL projects (any depth) 129 mise //...:test 130 131 # Run 'build' in all subdirs under projects/ 132 mise //projects/...:build 133 134 # Match paths with wildcards in the middle 135 mise //projects/.../api:build # Matches projects/*/api and projects/*/*/api 136 ``` 137 138 ::: info 139 Additional glob patterns may be added in a future version so `mise //projects/*:build` 140 and `mise '//projects/**:build'` will likely be supported. We're using `...` because it matches 141 how bazel and buck2 do it. 142 ::: 143 144 #### Task Name Wildcards (`*`) 145 146 Use asterisk (`*`) to match task names: 147 148 ```bash 149 # Run ALL tasks in frontend project 150 mise '//projects/frontend:*' 151 152 # Run all tasks starting with 'test:' 153 mise '//projects/frontend:test:*' 154 155 # Run 'lint' task across all projects 156 mise //...:lint 157 ``` 158 159 ### Combining Wildcards 160 161 You can combine both types of wildcards for powerful patterns: 162 163 ```bash 164 # Run all tasks in all projects (idk why you'd ever want to do this, but you can) 165 mise '//...:*' 166 167 # Run all test tasks in all projects 168 mise '//...:test*' 169 170 # Run build tasks in all frontend-related projects 171 mise //.../frontend:build 172 ``` 173 174 ## Tool, Environment, and Vars Layering 175 176 Subdirectory tasks automatically use tools and environment variables from parent config files in the hierarchy. However, each subdirectory can also define its own tools and environment variables in its config_root. This allows you to: 177 178 1. Define common tools and environment at the monorepo root 179 2. Override tools or environment in specific subdirectories 180 3. Add additional tools or environment in subdirectories 181 182 `vars` follow the same hierarchy for task templating, so child config vars are available when 183 running subdirectory tasks from the monorepo root. 184 Child `task_config.includes` templates can also reference inherited vars, which is useful for 185 centralized task includes like `git::https://example.com/tasks.git//go.toml?ref={{vars.central_ref}}`. 186 187 ### Layering Example 188 189 ```toml 190 # /myproject/mise.toml 191 monorepo_root = true 192 193 [tools] 194 node = "20" # Available to all subdirectories 195 python = "3.12" # Available to all subdirectories 196 197 [env] 198 LOG_LEVEL = "info" # Available to all subdirectories 199 ``` 200 201 ```toml 202 # /myproject/projects/frontend/mise.toml 203 [tools] 204 node = "18" # Overrides the root's node 20 205 206 [env] 207 LOG_LEVEL = "debug" # Overrides the root's LOG_LEVEL 208 PORT = "3000" # Adds new environment variable 209 210 [tasks.build] 211 run = "npm run build" # Uses node 18 and LOG_LEVEL=debug 212 ``` 213 214 ```toml 215 # /myproject/projects/backend/mise.toml 216 # No tools or env section - uses node 20, python 3.12, and LOG_LEVEL=info from root 217 218 [tasks.build] 219 run = "npm run build" # Uses node 20 and LOG_LEVEL=info from root 220 ```

     1	# mise.lock Lockfile
     2	
     3	`mise.lock` is a lockfile that pins exact versions and checksums of tools for reproducible environments. Lockfiles are not created automatically—you must run `mise lock` to generate them. Once a lockfile exists, mise will keep it updated as tools are installed or upgraded.
     4	
     5	## Overview
     6	
     7	The lockfile serves similar purposes to `package-lock.json` in npm or `Cargo.lock` in Rust:
     8	
     9	- **Reproducible builds**: Ensures everyone on your team uses exactly the same tool versions
    10	- **Security**: Verifies tool integrity with checksums when supported by the backend
    11	- **Version pinning**: Locks tools to specific versions while allowing flexibility in `mise.toml`
    12	- **Avoids API rate limits**: By storing download URLs, future installs use the lockfile and do not need to call GitHub (or other providers), avoiding rate limits and the need for `GITHUB_TOKEN` in most cases
    13	
    14	## Enabling Lockfiles
    15	
    16	Lockfiles are controlled by the `lockfile` setting:
    17	
    18	```sh
    19	# Enable lockfiles globally
    20	mise settings lockfile=true
    21	
    22	# Or set in mise.toml
    23	[settings]
    24	lockfile = true
    25	```
    26	
    27	## How It Works
    28	
    29	1. **Lockfile Updates**: Once a `mise.lock` file exists, running `mise install` or `mise use` updates it with the exact versions installed
    30	2. **Version Resolution**: If a `mise.lock` exists, mise will prefer locked versions over version ranges in `mise.toml`
    31	3. **Checksum Verification**: For supported backends, mise stores and verifies checksums of downloaded tools
    32	
    33	## File Format
    34	
    35	`mise.lock` is a TOML file with a platform-based format that organizes asset information by platform:
    36	
    37	```toml
    38	# Example mise.lock
    39	[[tools.node]]
    40	version = "20.11.0"
    41	backend = "core:node"
    42	
    43	[tools.node.platforms.linux-x64]
    44	checksum = "sha256:a6c213b7a2c3b8b9c0aaf8d7f5b3a5c8d4e2f4a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7"
    45	size = 23456789
    46	url = "https://nodejs.org/dist/v20.11.0/node-v20.11.0-linux-x64.tar.xz"
    47	
    48	[[tools.python]]
    49	version = "3.11.7"
    50	backend = "core:python"
    51	
    52	[tools.python.platforms.linux-x64]
    53	checksum = "sha256:def456..."
    54	size = 12345678
    55	
    56	# Tool with backend-specific options
    57	[[tools.ripgrep]]
    58	version = "14.1.1"
    59	backend = "aqua:BurntSushi/ripgrep"
    60	options = { exe = "rg" }
    61	
    62	[tools.ripgrep.platforms.linux-x64]
    63	checksum = "sha256:4cf9f2741e6c465ffdb7c26f38056a59e2a2544b51f7cc128ef28337eeae4d8e"
    64	size = 1234567
    65	
    66	```
    67	
    68	### Platform Information
    69	
    70	Each platform in a tool's `[tools.name.platforms]` section uses a key format like `"os-arch"` (e.g., `"linux-x64"`, `"macos-arm64"`) and can contain:
    71	
    72	- **`checksum`** (optional): SHA256 or Blake3 hash for integrity verification
    73	- **`size`** (optional): File size in bytes for download validation
    74	- **`url`** (optional): Original download URL for reference or re-downloading
    75	
    76	### Tool Entry Fields
    77	
    78	Each tool entry (`[[tools.name]]`) can contain:
    79	
    80	- **`version`** (required): The exact version of the tool
    81	- **`backend`** (optional): The backend used to install the tool (e.g., `core:node`, `aqua:BurntSushi/ripgrep`)
    82	- **`options`** (optional): Backend-specific options that identify the artifact (e.g., `{exe = "rg", matching = "musl"}`)
    83	- **`platforms`** (optional): Platform-specific metadata (checksums, URLs, sizes)
    84	
    85	### Platform Keys
    86	
    87	The platform key format is generally `os-arch` but can be customized by backends:
    88	
    89	- **Standard format**: `linux-x64`, `macos-arm64`, `windows-x64`
    90	- **Backend-specific**: Some backends like Java may use more specific platform identifiers
    91	- **Tool-specific**: Backends like `ubi` may include additional tool-specific information in the platform key
    92	
    93	## Environment-Specific Lockfiles
    94	
    95	When using [environment-specific configuration files](/configuration/environments) (e.g., `mise.test.toml`), each environment gets its own lockfile:
    96	
    97	| Config file            | Lockfile               |
    98	| ---------------------- | ---------------------- |
    99	| `mise.toml`            | `mise.lock`            |
   100	| `mise.test.toml`       | `mise.test.lock`       |
   101	| `mise.staging.toml`    | `mise.staging.lock`    |
   102	| `mise.local.toml`      | `mise.local.lock`      |
   103	| `mise.test.local.toml` | `mise.test.local.lock` |
   104	
   105	For example, with `MISE_ENV=test`:
   106	
   107	```sh
   108	MISE_ENV=test mise lock  # creates mise.lock AND mise.test.lock
   109	```
   110	
   111	Tools from `mise.toml` go to `mise.lock`, tools from `mise.test.toml` go to `mise.test.lock`.
   112	
   113	**Resolution**: When `MISE_ENV=test`, mise reads `mise.test.lock` for tools defined in `mise.test.toml` and `mise.lock` for tools in `mise.toml`. Environment-specific lockfiles are strictly scoped to their corresponding config — they only contain tools defined in that config.
   114	
   115	This design means CI environments that don't set `MISE_ENV` only depend on `mise.lock`, so dev tool version bumps in `mise.dev.lock` won't invalidate CI caches.
   116	
   117	Both `mise.lock` and `mise.<env>.lock` files should be committed to version control. `mise.local.lock` and `mise.<env>.local.lock` should be gitignored alongside their corresponding config files.
   118	
   119	## Local Lockfiles
   120	
   121	Tools defined in `mise.local.toml` (which is typically gitignored) use a separate `mise.local.lock` file. This keeps local tool configurations separate from the committed lockfile.
   122	
   123	```sh
   124	# mise.local.toml tools go to mise.local.lock
   125	mise use --path mise.local.toml node@22
   126	
   127	# Regular mise.toml tools go to mise.lock
   128	mise use --path mise.toml node@20
   129	```
   130	
   131	Use `mise lock --local` to update the local lockfile for all platforms:
   132	
   133	```sh
   134	mise lock --local              # update mise.local.lock
   135	mise lock --local node python  # update specific tools in mise.local.lock
   136	```
   137	
   138	## Strict Lockfile Mode
   139	
   140	The `locked` setting enforces that all tools have pre-resolved URLs in the lockfile before installation. This prevents API calls to GitHub, aqua registry, etc., ensuring fully reproducible installations.
   141	
   142	```sh
   143	# Enable strict mode
   144	mise settings locked=true
   145	
   146	# Or via environment variable
   147	MISE_LOCKED=1 mise install
   148	```
   149	
   150	::: warning
   151	All mise settings are global in scope. Setting `locked = true` in a project's `mise.toml` applies to **all** tool resolution, including tools from your global `~/.config/mise/config.toml`. If you see warnings about global tools missing from the lockfile, run `mise lock -g` to generate a global lockfile.
   152	:::
   153	
   154	When enabled, `mise install` will fail if a tool doesn't have a URL for the current platform in the lockfile. To fix this, first populate the lockfile with URLs:
   155	
   156	```sh
   157	mise lock                    # generate URLs for all platforms
   158	mise lock --platform linux-x64,macos-arm64  # or specific platforms
   159	```
   160	
   161	This is useful for CI environments where you want to guarantee reproducible builds without any external API dependencies.
   162	
   163	## Workflow
   164	
   165	### Initial Setup
   166	
   167	```sh
   168	# Generate the lockfile
   169	mise lock
   170	
   171	# Install tools using locked versions
   172	mise install
   173	```
   174	
   175	### Daily Usage
   176	
   177	```sh
   178	# Install exact versions from lockfile
   179	mise install
   180	
   181	# Update tools and lockfile
   182	mise upgrade
   183	```
   184	
   185	### Updating Versions
   186	
   187	When you want to update tool versions:
   188	
   189	```sh
   190	# Update tool version in mise.toml
   191	mise use node@26
   192	
   193	# This will update both the installation and mise.lock
   194	```
   195	
   196	### Pinning a Locked Version
   197	
   198	You can pin a specific version in the lockfile while keeping a fuzzy specifier in `mise.toml`:
   199	
   200	```sh
   201	# mise.toml has node = "latest" or node = "22"
   202	mise upgrade node@22.15.0   # installs 22.15.0 and updates mise.lock
   203	mise lock node@22.15.0      # updates mise.lock without reinstalling
   204	```
   205	
   206	If the version doesn't match the current config prefix, the config is updated automatically. For example, if `mise.toml` has `node = "20"` and you run `mise upgrade node@22.15.0`, the config is bumped to `node = "22"` (preserving the same precision level) and the lockfile is set to `22.15.0`.
   207	
   208	## Command Behavior with Lockfiles
   209	
   210	The table below shows how each command interacts with `mise.toml` and `mise.lock`:
   211	
   212	| Command                     | Installs | Updates `mise.toml`                  | Updates `mise.lock`                     |
   213	| --------------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------- |
   214	| `mise use node@22`          | Yes      | Yes (sets `node = "22"`)             | Yes                                     |
   215	| `mise install`              | Yes      | No                                   | Yes                                     |
   216	| `mise install node`         | Yes      | No                                   | Yes (installs config version for node)  |
   217	| `mise install node@22.15.0` | Yes      | No                                   | No (one-off install, not config-driven) |
   218	| `mise upgrade`              | Yes      | No                                   | Yes                                     |
   219	| `mise upgrade node`         | Yes      | No                                   | Yes (upgrades node within its range)    |
   220	| `mise upgrade node@22.15.0` | Yes      | Only if version doesn't match prefix | Yes                                     |

payload.message: One more thing I’m tracing is lockfile reads, not just writes. If unified root lockfiles are enabled, every reader that derives a path from a subproject config has to resolve to the root variant; if disabled, those same call sites must keep today’s colocated behavior.

content.text: One more thing I’m tracing is lockfile reads, not just writes. If unified root lockfiles are enabled, every reader that derives a path from a subproject config has to resolve to the root variant; if disabled, those same call sites must keep today’s colocated behavior.

payload.message: The filesystem lock helper already exists and is suitable for serializing the shared root lockfile writes. The plan will call it out for both install-time writes and migration; `mise lock` writes are worth including too because it also mutates the same root file.

payload.message: The current `mise lock` project-root e2e explicitly expects monorepo lock scope to follow the active config root. Since the requested change makes monorepo locks root-wide by default, that test will need to be updated and complemented with an opt-out case for `[monorepo] lockfile = false`.

content.text: The current `mise lock` project-root e2e explicitly expects monorepo lock scope to follow the active config root. Since the requested change makes monorepo locks root-wide by default, that test will need to be updated and complemented with an opt-out case for `[monorepo] lockfile = false`.

    14	# Portable timeout helper for e2e tests.
    15	# Uses system timeout/gtimeout when available, otherwise falls back to a
    16	# shell implementation that returns 124 on timeout.
    17	run_with_timeout() {
    18	  local seconds="$1"
    19	  shift
    20	
    21	  local output_file
    22	  output_file="$(mktemp)"
    23	
    24	  ("$@" >"$output_file" 2>&1) &
    25	  local cmd_pid=$!
    26	  local elapsed=0
    27	  local timed_out=0
    28	
    29	  while kill -0 "$cmd_pid" 2>/dev/null; do
    30	    if ((elapsed >= seconds * 10)); then
    31	      timed_out=1
    32	      kill -TERM "$cmd_pid" 2>/dev/null || true
    33	      sleep 0.2
    34	      kill -KILL "$cmd_pid" 2>/dev/null || true
    35	      wait "$cmd_pid" 2>/dev/null || true
    36	      break
    37	    fi
    38	    sleep 0.1
    39	    elapsed=$((elapsed + 1))
    40	  done
    41	
    42	  local status=0
    43	  if ((timed_out == 1)); then
    44	    status=124
    45	  else
    46	    wait "$cmd_pid" || status=$?
    47	  fi
    48	
    49	  cat "$output_file"
    50	  rm -f "$output_file"
    51	  return "$status"
    52	}
    53	
    54	parse_timeout_seconds() {
    55	  local duration="$1"
    56	  if [[ ! $duration =~ ^[0-9]+[smh]?$ ]]; then
    57	    return 1
    58	  fi
    59	  case "$duration" in
    60	    *s)
    61	      echo "${duration%s}"
    62	      ;;
    63	    *m)
    64	      echo $((10#${duration%m} * 60))
    65	      ;;
    66	    *h)
    67	      echo $((10#${duration%h} * 3600))
    68	      ;;
    69	    *)
    70	      echo $((10#$duration))
    71	      ;;
    72	  esac
    73	}
    74	
    75	timeout() {
    76	  if type -P timeout >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    77	    command timeout "$@"
    78	    return $?
    79	  fi
    80	  if type -P gtimeout >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    81	    command gtimeout "$@"
    82	    return $?
    83	  fi
    84	  if [[ $# -lt 2 ]]; then
    85	    echo "timeout fallback requires DURATION and COMMAND" >&2
    86	    return 125
    87	  fi
    88	  local seconds
    89	  seconds="$(parse_timeout_seconds "$1")" || {
    90	    echo "unsupported timeout duration: $1" >&2
    91	    return 125
    92	  }
    93	  shift
    94	  run_with_timeout "$seconds" "$@"
    95	}
    96	
    97	wait_for_file() {
    98	  local file="$1"
    99	  local description="${2:-file}"
   100	  local attempts="${3:-30}"
   101	  local pid="${4:-}"
   102	
   103	  for ((i = 0; i < attempts; i++)); do
   104	    if [[ -f $file ]]; then
   105	      return 0
   106	    fi
   107	    if [[ -n $pid ]] && ! kill -0 "$pid" 2>/dev/null; then
   108	      fail "$description process $pid exited before writing $file"

docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:1:# mise.lock Lockfile
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:3:`mise.lock` is a lockfile that pins exact versions and checksums of tools for reproducible environments. Lockfiles are not created automatically—you must run `mise lock` to generate them. Once a lockfile exists, mise will keep it updated as tools are installed or upgraded.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:7:The lockfile serves similar purposes to `package-lock.json` in npm or `Cargo.lock` in Rust:
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:29:1. **Lockfile Updates**: Once a `mise.lock` file exists, running `mise install` or `mise use` updates it with the exact versions installed
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:30:2. **Version Resolution**: If a `mise.lock` exists, mise will prefer locked versions over version ranges in `mise.toml`
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:35:`mise.lock` is a TOML file with a platform-based format that organizes asset information by platform:
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:38:# Example mise.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:99:| `mise.toml`            | `mise.lock`            |
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:100:| `mise.test.toml`       | `mise.test.lock`       |
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:101:| `mise.staging.toml`    | `mise.staging.lock`    |
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:102:| `mise.local.toml`      | `mise.local.lock`      |
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:103:| `mise.test.local.toml` | `mise.test.local.lock` |
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:108:MISE_ENV=test mise lock  # creates mise.lock AND mise.test.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:111:Tools from `mise.toml` go to `mise.lock`, tools from `mise.test.toml` go to `mise.test.lock`.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:113:**Resolution**: When `MISE_ENV=test`, mise reads `mise.test.lock` for tools defined in `mise.test.toml` and `mise.lock` for tools in `mise.toml`. Environment-specific lockfiles are strictly scoped to their corresponding config — they only contain tools defined in that config.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:115:This design means CI environments that don't set `MISE_ENV` only depend on `mise.lock`, so dev tool version bumps in `mise.dev.lock` won't invalidate CI caches.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:117:Both `mise.lock` and `mise.<env>.lock` files should be committed to version control. `mise.local.lock` and `mise.<env>.local.lock` should be gitignored alongside their corresponding config files.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:121:Tools defined in `mise.local.toml` (which is typically gitignored) use a separate `mise.local.lock` file. This keeps local tool configurations separate from the committed lockfile.
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:124:# mise.local.toml tools go to mise.local.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:127:# Regular mise.toml tools go to mise.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:134:mise lock --local              # update mise.local.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:135:mise lock --local node python  # update specific tools in mise.local.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:193:# This will update both the installation and mise.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:202:mise upgrade node@22.15.0   # installs 22.15.0 and updates mise.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:203:mise lock node@22.15.0      # updates mise.lock without reinstalling
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:210:The table below shows how each command interacts with `mise.toml` and `mise.lock`:
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:212:| Command                     | Installs | Updates `mise.toml`                  | Updates `mise.lock`                     |
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:250:git add mise.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:257:2. **Team Lead**: Runs `mise install` to update `mise.lock`
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:267:    mise install  # Uses exact versions from mise.lock
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:272:    key: mise-lock-${{ hashFiles('mise.lock') }}
docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:305:1. Resolve conflicts in `mise.lock`

payload.message: The union request set path is important: `Toolset::from(ToolRequestSet)` preserves multiple requests under the same backend, while the ordinary config merge path replaces lower-precedence tools. So the install/ls monorepo union should be built as a `ToolRequestSet`, not as a single flattened config map.

content.text: The union request set path is important: `Toolset::from(ToolRequestSet)` preserves multiple requests under the same backend, while the ordinary config merge path replaces lower-precedence tools. So the install/ls monorepo union should be built as a `ToolRequestSet`, not as a single flattened config map.

payload.message: The requested `install --monorepo` and the existing monorepo task execution share a theme: some code builds a temporary toolset from configs that are not in the active `Config`. The lockfile path helpers need to derive monorepo routing from the root config, while callers that build temporary config maps must pass enough source paths for lock updates to know what to touch.

content.text: The requested `install --monorepo` and the existing monorepo task execution share a theme: some code builds a temporary toolset from configs that are not in the active `Config`. The lockfile path helpers need to derive monorepo routing from the root config, while callers that build temporary config maps must pass enough source paths for lock updates to know what to touch.

payload.message: I’m also noting a follow-on integration point: `mise run` can auto-install subproject tools using a temporary combined config map. The safest implementation is to make lockfile path resolution depend on the tool source path plus monorepo config policy, rather than assuming every contributing config is in `config.config_files`.

content.text: I’m also noting a follow-on integration point: `mise run` can auto-install subproject tools using a temporary combined config map. The safest implementation is to make lockfile path resolution depend on the tool source path plus monorepo config policy, rather than assuming every contributing config is in `config.config_files`.

payload.message: There is a subtle source-tracking issue with the opt-out: the current `ToolVersionList` carries one source per backend, even though individual requests also have sources. For per-subproject lockfiles, the plan needs to group lockfile updates by each resolved request’s source so a monorepo union does not accidentally attribute all `node` requests to whichever config was seen first.

content.text: There is a subtle source-tracking issue with the opt-out: the current `ToolVersionList` carries one source per backend, even though individual requests also have sources. For per-subproject lockfiles, the plan needs to group lockfile updates by each resolved request’s source so a monorepo union does not accidentally attribute all `node` requests to whichever config was seen first.

item.text:
# Monorepo Install And Lockfile Plan

## Summary
Implement the proposed `mise install --monorepo` and unified monorepo lockfile behavior, with an opt-out:

```toml
[monorepo]
lockfile = false
```

Default behavior in a monorepo is unified root lockfiles: subproject configs write/read `<monorepo_root>/mise.lock` variants. When `[monorepo] lockfile = false`, mise keeps current colocated lockfile behavior, so subprojects can have their own `mise.lock` files.

## Key Changes
- Add `MonorepoConfig.lockfile: Option<bool>` and a `Config` helper that returns unified-lockfile root only when `monorepo_root = true` and `[monorepo] lockfile` is not `false`.
- Add monorepo config-root helpers:
  - require explicit `[monorepo].config_roots` for full monorepo tool operations
  - load each root using active `MISE_ENV` config filenames only
  - build a union request set across roots while preserving request source paths for lockfile attribution
- Add `ToolRequestSetBuilder::with_config_files`, mirroring `ToolsetBuilder`.

## CLI Behavior
- Add `mise install --monorepo` with `MISE_MONOREPO`.
  - Bare install installs missing tools from every configured monorepo config root.
  - `mise install --monorepo node` installs every configured `node` request across roots.
  - Outside a monorepo, or without explicit `config_roots`, return a clear error.
- Add `mise ls --monorepo` with the same union request set for CI/cache inspection.
- Do not add `--monorepo` to upgrade/prune/outdated in this pass.

## Lockfile Behavior
- Centralize lockfile routing through helpers that accept the monorepo lockfile root.
  - Enabled/default: descendant config lockfiles route to root variants like `mise.lock`, `mise.ci.lock`, `mise.local.lock`.
  - Disabled: existing per-config-root routing remains unchanged.
- Update lockfile reads, install writes, auto-lock writes, conda/pkgx lockfile reads, and tracked-config lock protection to use the centralized routing.
- Change `update_lockfiles` to group resolved versions by each `ToolVersion.request.source()` instead of the backend-level `ToolVersionList.source`, so monorepo union installs preserve correct subproject attribution when unified lockfiles are disabled.
- For unified root lockfile writes, use upsert-only install semantics: preserve existing root entries absent from the current partial install view. Pruning happens only through full-view `mise lock`.
- Add migration only when unified root lockfiles are enabled:
  - merge existing subproject `mise*.lock` files into root variants
  - root entries win on conflicts
  - delete migrated subproject locks
  - skip migration entirely when `[monorepo] lockfile = false`
- Make `mise lock` automatically full-view in monorepos only when unified root lockfiles are enabled. With `[monorepo] lockfile = false`, keep current active-config-root scope.

## Tests
- Unit tests:
  - `[monorepo] lockfile` default/false parsing
  - lockfile path routing enabled vs disabled
  - monorepo union keeps distinct versions across roots and preserves within-root override precedence
  - update-lockfile upsert preserves sibling root entries
  - migration merges root locks and skips when disabled
- E2E tests:
  - `install --monorepo` bare and filtered installs
  - errors outside monorepo and without explicit `config_roots`
  - unified root lockfile install/update/prune/migration behavior
  - `[monorepo] lockfile = false` keeps subproject lockfiles and avoids migration
  - update existing `test_lock_project_root_scope` expectations for new default full-view monorepo lock behavior
- Verification:
  - `mise run build`
  - focused unit tests for config/tool_request_set/lockfile
  - targeted e2e tests for install and lockfile monorepo flows
  - `mise run render:usage` for CLI docs
  - `mise run lint-fix`

## Assumptions
- `[monorepo] lockfile` is project config only; no new env var is added for that opt-out.
- Default remains unified root lockfile because that is the desired monorepo behavior.
- Task monorepo discovery fallback remains unchanged; only full-view tool/lock operations require explicit `config_roots`.

<proposed_plan>
# Monorepo Install And Lockfile Plan

## Summary
Implement the proposed `mise install --monorepo` and unified monorepo lockfile behavior, with an opt-out:

```toml
[monorepo]
lockfile = false
```

Default behavior in a monorepo is unified root lockfiles: subproject configs write/read `<monorepo_root>/mise.lock` variants. When `[monorepo] lockfile = false`, mise keeps current colocated lockfile behavior, so subprojects can have their own `mise.lock` files.

## Key Changes
- Add `MonorepoConfig.lockfile: Option<bool>` and a `Config` helper that returns unified-lockfile root only when `monorepo_root = true` and `[monorepo] lockfile` is not `false`.
- Add monorepo config-root helpers:
  - require explicit `[monorepo].config_roots` for full monorepo tool operations
  - load each root using active `MISE_ENV` config filenames only
  - build a union request set across roots while preserving request source paths for lockfile attribution
- Add `ToolRequestSetBuilder::with_config_files`, mirroring `ToolsetBuilder`.

## CLI Behavior
- Add `mise install --monorepo` with `MISE_MONOREPO`.
  - Bare install installs missing tools from every configured monorepo config root.
  - `mise install --monorepo node` installs every configured `node` request across roots.
  - Outside a monorepo, or without explicit `config_roots`, return a clear error.
- Add `mise ls --monorepo` with the same union request set for CI/cache inspection.
- Do not add `--monorepo` to upgrade/prune/outdated in this pass.

## Lockfile Behavior
- Centralize lockfile routing through helpers that accept the monorepo lockfile root.
  - Enabled/default: descendant config lockfiles route to root variants like `mise.lock`, `mise.ci.lock`, `mise.local.lock`.
  - Disabled: existing per-config-root routing remains unchanged.
- Update lockfile reads, install writes, auto-lock writes, conda/pkgx lockfile reads, and tracked-config lock protection to use the centralized routing.
- Change `update_lockfiles` to group resolved versions by each `ToolVersion.request.source()` instead of the backend-level `ToolVersionList.source`, so monorepo union installs preserve correct subproject attribution when unified lockfiles are disabled.
- For unified root lockfile writes, use upsert-only install semantics: preserve existing root entries absent from the current partial install view. Pruning happens only through full-view `mise lock`.
- Add migration only when unified root lockfiles are enabled:
  - merge existing subproject `mise*.lock` files into root variants
  - root entries win on conflicts
  - delete migrated subproject locks
  - skip migration entirely when `[monorepo] lockfile = false`
- Make `mise lock` automatically full-view in monorepos only when unified root lockfiles are enabled. With `[monorepo] lockfile = false`, keep current active-config-root scope.

## Tests
- Unit tests:
  - `[monorepo] lockfile` default/false parsing
  - lockfile path routing enabled vs disabled
  - monorepo union keeps distinct versions across roots and preserves within-root override precedence
  - update-lockfile upsert preserves sibling root entries
  - migration merges root locks and skips when disabled
- E2E tests:
  - `install --monorepo` bare and filtered installs
  - errors outside monorepo and without explicit `config_roots`
  - unified root lockfile install/update/prune/migration behavior
  - `[monorepo] lockfile = false` keeps subproject lockfiles and avoids migration
  - update existing `test_lock_project_root_scope` expectations for new default full-view monorepo lock behavior
- Verification:
  - `mise run build`
  - focused unit tests for config/tool_request_set/lockfile
  - targeted e2e tests for install and lockfile monorepo flows
  - `mise run render:usage` for CLI docs
  - `mise run lint-fix`

## Assumptions
- `[monorepo] lockfile` is project config only; no new env var is added for that opt-out.
- Default remains unified root lockfile because that is the desired monorepo behavior.
- Task monorepo discovery fallback remains unchanged; only full-view tool/lock operations require explicit `config_roots`.
</proposed_plan>

settings.developer_instructions:
# Plan Mode (Conversational)

You work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.

## Mode rules (strict)

You are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.

Plan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.

## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool

Plan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.

Separately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.

## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode

You may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.

### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)

Actions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs
* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration
* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files
* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files

### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)

Actions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Editing or writing files
* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files
* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files
* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it

When in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as "doing the work" rather than "planning the work," do not do it.

## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)

Begin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.

Before asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.

Exception: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.

Do not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, "where is this struct?" or "which UI component should we use?" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.

## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)

* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.
* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.

## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)

* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.

## Asking questions

Critical rules:

* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.
* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.
* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.

You SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:

* materially change the spec/plan, OR
* confirm/lock an assumption, OR
* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.
* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.

Use the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.

## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)

1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.

   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).
   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.
   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.
   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).

2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.

   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.
   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.
   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.

## Finalization rule

Only output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.

When you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:

1) The opening tag must be on its own line.
2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).
3) The closing tag must be on its own line.
4) Use Markdown inside the block.
5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.

Example:

<proposed_plan>
plan content
</proposed_plan>

plan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:

* A clear title
* A brief summary section
* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types
* Test cases and scenarios
* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed

When possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.

Prefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.

Keep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.

Do not ask "should I proceed?" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.

Only produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.

If the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement. If the user indicates that the prior plan is not acceptable but does not provide enough information to produce a complete replacement, address the concern and continue planning without producing a `<proposed_plan>` block. If the follow-up neither requires changes nor calls the plan into question (e.g. clarifying question), answer it before the block, then reproduce the prior `<proposed_plan>` unchanged.

content.text: Yes, answering that first. Under the plan as written, migration would be automatic, but only for projects that are actually in unified monorepo-lockfile mode.

payload.message:
Concretely, I’d make new mise auto-migrate **before the first lockfile-dependent operation that could rely on those locks**, not only after an install has already resolved versions. That means `mise lock`, `mise install`, `mise use`, `mise upgrade`, and auto-install paths should trigger migration early enough that the first run after upgrading still sees the old per-subproject lock data.

For a monorepo with existing subproject locks and no opt-out:

- `packages/a/mise.lock` merges into `<monorepo_root>/mise.lock`.
- `packages/a/mise.ci.lock` merges into `<monorepo_root>/mise.ci.lock`.
- `packages/a/mise.local.lock` merges into `<monorepo_root>/mise.local.lock`.
- Existing root entries win on conflicts; unique subproject entries are appended.
- The migrated subproject lockfiles are deleted.
- A notice should be printed once for the run, like “migrated N subproject lockfile(s) into <root>”.

If the project sets:

```toml
[monorepo]
lockfile = false
```

then none of that happens. New mise keeps reading/writing lockfiles beside each subproject config, preserving old behavior.

Older mise versions are the compatibility problem. They do not know that a subproject config’s lock entries now live in the monorepo root. After migration and deletion of subproject lockfiles, old mise run from a subproject will generally **not respect the migrated root lockfile entries for that subproject’s own tools**. It will resolve from the loose version in `mise.toml`; in strict locked flows it may fail because the colocated lockfile it expects is gone. If someone runs old `mise lock` in a subproject, it may recreate a subproject `mise.lock`, and new mise would later treat that as a stray lock to migrate again.

So yes, the practical answer is: older mise will mostly not respect the new unified lockfile for subproject-owned tool entries. Teams that need mixed-version compatibility should use `[monorepo] lockfile = false` during the transition, or bump/pin the project’s required mise version when adopting unified root lockfiles.

I’ll also fold in your stub request: add `--monorepo` flags to the deferred commands from the PR, with `unimplemented!()` bodies rather than silently ignoring the flag.

item.text:
# Monorepo Install, Lockfiles, And Stubs

## Summary
Add `mise install --monorepo`, `mise ls --monorepo`, and unified monorepo root lockfiles by default. Add the project opt-out:

```toml
[monorepo]
lockfile = false
```

When the opt-out is set, mise keeps today’s per-subproject `mise.lock` behavior.

## Key Changes
- Add `MonorepoConfig.lockfile: Option<bool>`; default `None`/`true` means route descendant config locks to the monorepo root, `false` keeps colocated locks.
- Add monorepo union helpers requiring explicit `[monorepo].config_roots` for full-view operations.
- Add `ToolRequestSetBuilder::with_config_files` and build monorepo unions as request sets so `node@20` and `node@22` across roots both survive.
- Add CLI support:
  - `install --monorepo` installs the union across config roots; filtered args install every matching request.
  - `ls --monorepo` lists/resolves the same union.

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+
+export MISE_EXPERIMENTAL=1
+
+ln -sf "$ROOT/test/data/plugins/tiny" "$MISE_DATA_DIR/plugins/tiny"
+ln -sf "$ROOT/test/data/plugins/dummy" "$MISE_DATA_DIR/plugins/dummy"
+
+echo "=== install --monorepo installs every configured root ==="
+mkdir -p monorepo/packages/a monorepo/packages/b
+cat >monorepo/mise.toml <<'EOF'
+monorepo_root = true
+
+[monorepo]
+config_roots = ["packages/*"]
+EOF
+cat >monorepo/packages/a/mise.toml <<'EOF'
+[tools]
+tiny = "1.0.0"
+EOF
+cat >monorepo/packages/b/mise.toml <<'EOF'
+[tools]
+tiny = "2.1.0"
+dummy = "1.0.0"
+EOF
+
+assert "cd monorepo && mise install --monorepo"
+assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/1.0.0'"
+assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/2.1.0'"
+assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/dummy/1.0.0'"
+
+echo "=== install --monorepo TOOL filters across roots ==="
+rm -rf "$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny" "$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/dummy"
+assert "cd monorepo && mise install --monorepo tiny"
+assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/1.0.0'"
+assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/2.1.0'"
+assert_fail "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/dummy/1.0.0'"
+
+echo "=== ls --monorepo includes every configured root ==="
+output=$(cd monorepo && mise ls --monorepo 2>&1)
+assert_contains "echo '$output'" "tiny"
+assert_contains "echo '$output'" "1.0.0"
+assert_contains "echo '$output'" "2.1.0"
+assert_contains "echo '$output'" "dummy"
+
+echo "=== --monorepo requires monorepo_root and explicit config_roots ==="
+mkdir -p outside no-roots
+cat >no-roots/mise.toml <<'EOF'
+monorepo_root = true

+assert_fail_contains "cd outside && mise install --monorepo 2>&1" "monorepo_root = true"

+assert_fail_contains "cd monorepo && mise prune --monorepo 2>&1" "not implemented"
+assert_fail_contains "cd monorepo && mise outdated --monorepo 2>&1" "not implemented"

#!/usr/bin/env bash

export MISE_EXPERIMENTAL=1

ln -sf "$ROOT/test/data/plugins/tiny" "$MISE_DATA_DIR/plugins/tiny"
ln -sf "$ROOT/test/data/plugins/dummy" "$MISE_DATA_DIR/plugins/dummy"

echo "=== install --monorepo installs every configured root ==="
mkdir -p monorepo/packages/a monorepo/packages/b
cat >monorepo/mise.toml <<'EOF'
monorepo_root = true

[monorepo]
config_roots = ["packages/*"]
EOF
cat >monorepo/packages/a/mise.toml <<'EOF'
[tools]
tiny = "1.0.0"
EOF
cat >monorepo/packages/b/mise.toml <<'EOF'
[tools]
tiny = "2.1.0"
dummy = "1.0.0"
EOF

assert "cd monorepo && mise install --monorepo"
assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/1.0.0'"
assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/2.1.0'"
assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/dummy/1.0.0'"

echo "=== install --monorepo TOOL filters across roots ==="
rm -rf "$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny" "$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/dummy"
assert "cd monorepo && mise install --monorepo tiny"
assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/1.0.0'"
assert "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/tiny/2.1.0'"
assert_fail "test -d '$MISE_DATA_DIR/installs/dummy/1.0.0'"

echo "=== ls --monorepo includes every configured root ==="
output=$(cd monorepo && mise ls --monorepo 2>&1)
assert_contains "echo '$output'" "tiny"
assert_contains "echo '$output'" "1.0.0"
assert_contains "echo '$output'" "2.1.0"
assert_contains "echo '$output'" "dummy"

echo "=== --monorepo requires monorepo_root and explicit config_roots ==="
mkdir -p outside no-roots
cat >no-roots/mise.toml <<'EOF'
monorepo_root = true
EOF
assert_fail_contains "cd outside && mise install --monorepo 2>&1" "monorepo_root = true"
assert_fail_contains "cd no-roots && mise install --monorepo 2>&1" "[monorepo].config_roots is required"

echo "=== deferred --monorepo commands are explicit stubs ==="
assert_fail_contains "cd monorepo && mise upgrade --monorepo 2>&1" "not implemented"
assert_fail_contains "cd monorepo && mise prune --monorepo 2>&1" "not implemented"
assert_fail_contains "cd monorepo && mise outdated --monorepo 2>&1" "not implemented"

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/jdx/.codex/worktrees/6b3b/mise/docs/tasks/monorepo.md
@@
 ## Tool, Environment, and Vars Layering
 
 Subdirectory tasks automatically use tools and environment variables from parent config files in the hierarchy. However, each subdirectory can also define its own tools and environment variables in its config_root. This allows you to:
@@
 [tasks.build]
 run = "npm run build"  # Uses node 20 and LOG_LEVEL=info
 ```
+
+## Tools
+
+Use `mise install --monorepo` to install the union of tools from every directory listed in `[monorepo].config_roots`. This is useful in CI when you want to warm a cache for all projects in the repository:
+
+```bash
+MISE_ENV=ci mise install --monorepo
+```
+
+Passing a tool name filters the union while preserving multiple configured versions:
+
+```bash
+mise install --monorepo node
+```
+
+`mise ls --monorepo` lists the same union and can be used to inspect cache keys or debug which config roots are contributing tools. Both commands require `monorepo_root = true` and explicit `[monorepo].config_roots`.
+
+## Lockfiles
+
+By default, monorepos use one lockfile at the monorepo root. Tools from `packages/api/mise.toml` write to `<monorepo_root>/mise.lock`, while environment and local variants write to root files such as `mise.ci.lock` and `mise.local.lock`.
+
+If mise finds old subproject lockfiles, it migrates them into the root lockfile the next time a lock-aware command runs. Root entries win on conflicts, unique subproject entries are preserved, and migrated subproject lockfiles are removed.
+
+To keep lockfiles next to each subproject config, opt out in the monorepo root:
+
+```toml
+[monorepo]
+lockfile = false
+```
+
+Older mise versions do not understand unified monorepo lockfiles for subproject-owned tools. Teams that need mixed-version compatibility should use `lockfile = false` until everyone has upgraded.
 
 ### Layering Example
 
 ```toml
*** End Patch

*** Update File: /Users/jdx/.codex/worktrees/6b3b/mise/docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md
@@
 Use `mise lock --local` to update the local lockfile for all platforms:
 
 ```sh
 mise lock --local              # update mise.local.lock
 mise lock --local node python  # update specific tools in mise.local.lock
 ```
+
+## Monorepos
+
+When `monorepo_root = true`, mise uses a single lockfile at the monorepo root by default. Subproject configs listed in `[monorepo].config_roots` write to root lockfile variants such as `mise.lock`, `mise.ci.lock`, and `mise.local.lock`.
+
+Existing subproject lockfiles are migrated into the root lockfile on the next lock-aware command. Older mise versions do not understand this layout for subproject-owned tools, so projects that need mixed-version compatibility can opt out:
+
+```toml
+[monorepo]
+lockfile = false
+```
+
+See [Monorepo Tasks](/tasks/monorepo.html#lockfiles) for details.
 
 ## Strict Lockfile Mode
 
 The `locked` setting enforces that all tools have pre-resolved URLs in the lockfile before installation. This prevents API calls to GitHub, aqua registry, etc., ensuring fully reproducible installations.
*** End Patch

payload.message: The lockfile e2e found a real bug: `mise lock` full-view was using a merged `ToolsetBuilder`, which collapses multiple versions of the same backend across roots. I’m switching monorepo lock to build from the union request set, just like `install --monorepo`, so `tiny@1.0.0` and `tiny@2.1.0` both survive.

content.text: The lockfile e2e found a real bug: `mise lock` full-view was using a merged `ToolsetBuilder`, which collapses multiple versions of the same backend across roots. I’m switching monorepo lock to build from the union request set, just like `install --monorepo`, so `tiny@1.0.0` and `tiny@2.1.0` both survive.

# Files mentioned by the user:

## PR: mise install --monorepo + unified monorepo lockfile Context Implements both…: /Users/jdx/.codex/attachments/a954e20c-8b65-47a3-9c19-219b7be05c8f/pasted-text.txt

## My request for Codex:
i want to implement this. While I think it's good default behavior for monorepo, i think there should be an option to disable it to have mise.lock files inside.

i want you to stub out --monorepo to the other commands in this PR with unimplemented!() macros.

Answer me this question before continuing: what will the behavior be for projects with multiple lockfiles when they upgrade mise to the version with this change? will it auto-migrate? what will happen to older mise versions on the same project? is it just that they won't respect the lockfile?

PLEASE IMPLEMENT THIS PLAN:
# Monorepo Install, Lockfiles, And Stubs

## Summary
Add `mise install --monorepo`, `mise ls --monorepo`, and unified monorepo root lockfiles by default. Add the project opt-out:

```toml
[monorepo]
lockfile = false
```

When the opt-out is set, mise keeps today’s per-subproject `mise.lock` behavior.

## Key Changes
- Add `MonorepoConfig.lockfile: Option<bool>`; default `None`/`true` means route descendant config locks to the monorepo root, `false` keeps colocated locks.
- Add monorepo union helpers requiring explicit `[monorepo].config_roots` for full-view operations.
- Add `ToolRequestSetBuilder::with_config_files` and build monorepo unions as request sets so `node@20` and `node@22` across roots both survive.
- Add CLI support:
  - `install --monorepo` installs the union across config roots; filtered args install every matching request.
  - `ls --monorepo` lists/resolves the same union.
  - Add stub `--monorepo` flags to `upgrade`, `prune`, and `outdated`; if set, immediately call `unimplemented!()`. These stubs should be CLI-only, not `MISE_MONOREPO` env-bound yet.

## Lockfile Behavior
- Centralize lockfile path routing so all reads/writes use root variants when unified monorepo lockfiles are enabled.
- Auto-migrate existing subproject lockfiles before lock-aware mutating flows such as `mise lock`, `mise install`, and `mise upgrade` resolve/write lock data.
- Migration is enabled only when global lockfiles are enabled, monorepo unified lockfiles are enabled, and explicit `config_roots` are present.
- Migration maps variants one-for-one, e.g. `pkg/mise.lock` to root `mise.lock`, `pkg/mise.ci.lock` to root `mise.ci.lock`; root entries win on conflicts, unique subproject entries are appended, migrated subproject locks are deleted.
- Install-time lockfile updates use upsert-only semantics for unified root locks so partial subproject views do not delete sibling entries. Full pruning remains `mise lock` only.
- Document that older mise versions do not understand unified root lockfiles for subproject-owned tools; mixed-version teams should set `[monorepo] lockfile = false` until upgraded.

## Tests
- Unit tests for monorepo lockfile setting, routing enabled/disabled, union request preservation, upsert preservation, and migration skip/merge behavior.
- E2E tests for `install --monorepo`, `ls --monorepo`, default unified root lockfiles, migration, pruning via full-view `mise lock`, and `[monorepo] lockfile = false`.
- Update existing monorepo lock scope tests to expect full-view root lock behavior by default and old active-root behavior only with the opt-out.
- Add focused tests that `upgrade --monorepo`, `prune --monorepo`, and `outdated --monorepo` reach the `unimplemented!()` path.

## Verification
Run `mise run build`, focused config/tool_request_set/lockfile unit tests, targeted e2e tests for monorepo install/lockfile/stubs, `mise run render:usage`, and `mise run lint-fix`.
